content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>plugin-settings </user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-03-05</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

===========================

PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
Risposta onesta e precisa:

**Caricato automaticamente ad ogni sessione:**
| Cosa | Come | Stato |
|------|------|-------|
| `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` | Claude Code lo legge sempre | ✅ |
| `MEMORY.md` + vault context | Hook `SessionStart` lo inietta | ✅ |
| Skill (`/linkedin-post`, `/meeting-brief`, ecc.) | Disponibili da invocare | ✅ |

**NON caricato automaticamente:**
| Cosa | Perché |
|------|--------|
| `about-me.md`, `brand-voice.md`, ecc. | File di riferimento — li leggo quando servono, non sono iniettati all'avvio |
| Sub-agenti | Non sono "in esecuzione" — li spawno io durante la sessione quando servono |
| Plugin Obsidian (Dataview, Templater) | Sono di Obsidian, non di Claude Code — già configurati lì |

**In pratica:** apri Claude Code, io so chi sei, conosco Columbia Transport, il team, i progetti, le safety rules. Se dici `/vault-review` spawno un sub-agente. Se dici "scrivi email a Giulia" vado a leggere `brand-voice.md` e `team-members.md` in quel momento.

Non è tutto pre-caricato in memoria — è recuperato su richiesta. Questo è il modo corretto: carico solo quello che serve, quando serve.

Claude's Full Response to User:
Le fonti principali:

**Plugin Claude Code** (si installano da Claude Code stesso)
- Apri Claude Code → `/plugins` → marketplace ufficiale
- Hai già installato i migliori: `everything-claude-code`, `superpowers`, `claude-mem`, ecc.

**Skill di terze parti**
- **everything.claude.code** — la raccolta più grande, già attiva da te. Ha skill per quasi tutto (frontend, backend, Swift, Python, deployment, ecc.)
- **GitHub**: cerca `claude-code skills` o `claude code SKILL.md` — community in crescita

**Per il tuo uso specifico (business/productivity):**
Le skill più utili che non hai ancora e che esistono già:

| Skill | Dove trovarla |
|-------|--------------|
| Ricerca web + sintesi | Plugin `firecrawl` — già installato |
| Prospecting aziende | Apollo.io MCP — già connesso al tuo Claude |
| Calendario/email | Gmail + Google Calendar MCP — già connessi |
| Ricerca su Slack | Plugin `slack` — già installato |

**Onestà:** il marketplace è ancora giovane. Le skill più efficaci per il tuo lavoro specifico (logistica, freight forwarding) non esistono preconfezionate — le abbiamo costruite noi su misura oggi. Quelle valgono più di quelle generiche.

Vuoi che testi una delle connessioni già attive? Ad esempio Apollo.io per iniziare a fare prospecting clienti tessile.

Claude's Full Response to User:
No. Le skill che abbiamo creato oggi (linkedin-post, email-cliente, quote-template, meeting-brief, vault-review, compress-memory) sono file `.md` salvati localmente sul tuo Mac — **non appaiono nel marketplace dell'app**.

Le trovi solo qui nel terminale, digitando `/` seguito dal nome.

Il marketplace dell'app mostra solo i plugin/skill pubblicati ufficialmente da altri sviluppatori. Le tue sono custom, locali, private.

**Per usarle:** rimani sul terminale Claude Code, digita `/linkedin-post` (o qualsiasi altra) e partono.

Claude's Full Response to User:
Installi dal marketplace e sono subito disponibili — niente di extra da fare.

**Flusso:**
1. Nel terminale digita `/plugins` → apre il marketplace
2. Trovi il plugin → installi
3. Le skill del plugin appaiono subito nella lista `/`

**Unica eccezione:** alcuni plugin richiedono una connessione a un servizio esterno (es. Notion, Linear, Sentry) — in quel caso ti chiede di autenticarti una volta sola.

Per il tuo lavoro ti consiglio di guardare in particolare:
- `firecrawl` — naviga e scrape web (utile per ricerca prospect/mercati)
- `playwright` — automazione browser
- Qualsiasi plugin con "research" o "prospecting" nel nome

Hai già i plugin più utili installati. Prima di installarne altri, ti conviene testare quelli che hai — molti li hai attivi ma non hai mai usato.

From the observed session, I can see:
- User requested: "plugin-settings"
- Claude responded with an explanation in Italian about what gets auto-loaded vs what doesn't in Claude Code sessions

The response explains:
- What's automatically loaded each session: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md + vault context via SessionStart hook, Skills
- What's NOT automatically loaded: about-me.md, brand-voice.md (reference files read on demand), sub-agents (spawned when needed), Obsidian plugins (already configured in Obsidian)
- The approach is to load things on-demand rather than pre-loading everything

This was an informational/clarification request. The user asked about plugin settings and Claude explained the current setup - what gets loaded automatically vs on-demand in their workspace.

  <investigated>Claude explained the current workspace configuration, distinguishing between auto-loaded resources and on-demand resources in the Columbia Transport setup</investigated>
  <learned>The workspace uses a hybrid loading strategy: core context files (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, MEMORY.md, vault context via SessionStart hook) are injected automatically at session start, while reference files (about-me.md, brand-voice.md, team-members.md) and sub-agents are loaded on-demand when needed. Skills are available to invoke but not pre-loaded. Obsidian plugins (Dataview, Templater) operate independently in Obsidian, not in Claude Code</learned>

  <notes>This was a meta-discussion about the workspace configuration itself rather than active development work. The on-demand loading approach is intentional to optimize memory usage - only retrieving resources when contextually relevant rather than injecting everything upfront</notes>
</summary>

Claude's Full Response to User:
Installi dal marketplace e sono subito disponibili — niente di extra da fare.

**Flusso:**
1. Nel terminale digita `/plugins` → apre il marketplace
2. Trovi il plugin → installi
3. Le skill del plugin appaiono subito nella lista `/`

**Unica eccezione:** alcuni plugin richiedono una connessione a un servizio esterno (es. Notion, Linear, Sentry) — in quel caso ti chiede di autenticarti una volta sola.

Per il tuo lavoro ti consiglio di guardare in particolare:
- `firecrawl` — naviga e scrape web (utile per ricerca prospect/mercati)
- `playwright` — automazione browser
- Qualsiasi plugin con "research" o "prospecting" nel nome

Hai già i plugin più utili installati. Prima di installarne altri, ti conviene testare quelli che hai — molti li hai attivi ma non hai mai usato.
